name: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)
description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a hybrid technique combining natural language generation with information retrieval. In this approach, a language model generates text based on retrieved documents, providing more accurate and relevant responses, especially for knowledge-intensive tasks. RAG allows large language models to enhance their outputs with real-time, domain-specific information. While this technique holds promise, especially in customer service and knowledge management systems, it’s still evolving, and its full potential is being explored. We recommend assessing RAG in applications where real-time data retrieval is critical for accuracy.

name: Text to DSL
description: As natural language processing improves, converting plain text into a Domain-Specific Language (DSL) is becoming an increasingly feasible technique. Text to DSL allows developers to transform written specifications or requirements into executable code, making it easier to bridge the gap between non-technical stakeholders and development teams. This technique is promising in environments that rely heavily on domain modeling, such as financial or regulatory software. However, as it’s still an emerging field, it requires robust models and domain-specific training. We recommend assessing this technique for organizations looking to streamline communication between business and engineering teams.

name: Spectral
description: Spectral is a JSON/YAML linter with an emphasis on OpenAPI and AsyncAPI specifications. It ships with a comprehensive set of out-of-the-box rules for these specs that can save developers headaches when designing and implementing APIs or event-driven collaboration. These rules check for proper API parameter specifications or the existence of a license statement in the spec, among other things. The CLI makes it easy to incorporate Spectral into both local development and CI/CD pipelines, and the JavaScript API supports more advanced use cases. The GitHub site links to publicly available real-world rule sets from companies like Adidas, giving teams a head start on adopting their own linting rules.

name: APISIX
description: 42Crunch API Conformance Scan is a dynamic testing tool designed to identify discrepancies between your API's documented behavior and its actual implementation. This tool takes your API's spec definition in OpenAPI format, which outlines the expected functionalities and responses, and compares it to the API's actual behavior. By generating real traffic and interacting with live endpoints, the tool can identify any discrepancies between what the API promises and what it delivers. This translates into several benefits for development teams. For example, it catches inconsistencies early in development, saving time and preventing issues from reaching production. The tool also helps improve API quality and security by identifying potential vulnerabilities arising from deviations from the documented behavior. Overall, API Scan helps you assess the security posture of your APIs by identifying problems such as weak authentication protocols, insecure data handling practices and insufficient input validation. It provides detailed reports highlighting any issues found, along with recommendations for remediation.

name: ADRs (Architectural Decision Records)
description: Architectural Decision Records (ADRs) are a method of capturing and documenting key architectural decisions during the development process. They ensure that the rationale behind decisions is well-documented and traceable, improving long-term maintainability and team alignment. By writing ADRs, teams can maintain a clear record of why certain choices were made, which is valuable for onboarding and future changes. While writing ADRs can seem like overhead in the short term, they provide significant long-term benefits in complex projects. We recommend adopting ADRs for teams working on projects with evolving architectures or high stakeholder involvement.

name: React Propmpting
description: ReAct prompting is a method for prompting LLMs intended to improve the accuracy of their responses over competing methods such as chain-of-thought (CoT). Introduced in a 2022 paper, it works by bringing together reasoning and action (hence ReAct). Such an approach helps make LLM responses more explainable and reduces hallucinations compared to CoT, giving prompters a better chance of getting what they want. LangChain was originally developed to support this style of prompting. Autonomous agents based on ReAct prompting have proven to be some of the most widely used applications of LLMs our teams have been building. Recently, OpenAI introduced function calling to its APIs to make ReAct and similar prompting styles easier to implement without resorting to external tools like LangChain. We're still in the early stages of defining this discipline, but so far, ReAct and its descendants have pointed the way to some of the most exciting applications of LLMs.

name: FastAPI
description: With the increasing demand for high-performance APIs in web development, FastAPI has emerged as a compelling solution. This Python-based web framework leverages asynchronous programming to enable rapid development of APIs while ensuring high performance. FastAPI comes with built-in support for data validation, automatic interactive API documentation, and minimal boilerplate code. These features make it particularly attractive for microservices and other performance-critical applications. Its asynchronous nature allows developers to handle a large number of concurrent requests efficiently. However, for teams familiar with more traditional synchronous frameworks like Flask or Django, transitioning may involve a learning curve. Given its benefits, FastAPI is worth trialing in scenarios where speed and API performance are crucial.

name: Langchain
description: As the adoption of large language models (LLMs) grows, Langchain provides an essential framework to facilitate integration of LLMs with external data sources, enabling more powerful and context-aware AI applications. Langchain offers an easy-to-use interface to build applications that chain LLMs together with tools such as databases or APIs. This allows developers to create AI systems that are not just limited to pre-trained knowledge but can query real-time data for up-to-date responses. While it’s gaining popularity, Langchain is still an evolving technology with frequent updates and changes. Organizations exploring advanced AI solutions should consider assessing Langchain, particularly in applications like chatbots, information retrieval, and automated decision-making.

name: LlamaIndex
description: LlamaIndex is designed to solve one of the key challenges in working with LLMs—efficiently indexing and querying large datasets. It enables developers to build complex, structured data indexes that large language models can then query in an efficient manner. LlamaIndex integrates seamlessly with other AI tools and platforms, making it a valuable asset for creating knowledge retrieval systems powered by LLMs. While still relatively new, its capacity to reduce query response times and enable more intelligent retrieval makes it an exciting development for organizations building scalable AI solutions. We recommend trialing LlamaIndex in knowledge management and enterprise search applications where query speed and data structure are critical.

name: Langfuse
description: Langfuse is a platform aimed at helping organizations track and monitor the performance of applications built on large language models. By providing observability tools specifically tailored to LLMs, Langfuse allows developers to better understand model performance, user interactions, and system behaviors in real-time. This platform is particularly useful for debugging, optimization, and compliance in AI applications. While still relatively new, Langfuse addresses a growing need for transparency and accountability in AI systems. We recommend trialing Langfuse for teams heavily invested in LLM-powered products.

name: Design systems
description: Design Systems standardize the design process across teams by providing a set of reusable UI components, design principles, and guidelines. They help maintain visual and functional consistency, reducing design debt and speeding up development by offering pre-defined components. Design Systems are particularly useful in organizations with large, cross-functional teams working on different parts of the product. However, maintaining a design system requires dedicated effort and alignment between design and engineering. We recommend adopting Design Systems in larger organizations that require consistency across multiple teams and products.

name: pvector
description: pgvector is a PostgreSQL extension that brings vector similarity search capabilities to the popular relational database. As machine learning models and AI applications increasingly rely on vector embeddings for tasks like recommendation systems, pgvector enables teams to store and query these embeddings directly in PostgreSQL. This avoids the need for separate vector databases and allows for seamless integration with existing infrastructure. While performance may be an issue at scale compared to dedicated vector search platforms, pgvector is a promising option for smaller-scale projects or teams already leveraging PostgreSQL. We recommend assessing pgvector for teams looking to experiment with vector search within a familiar database ecosystem.
